Introduction
When building modern React applications, one of the most common tasks is communicating with a backend API. Whether you‚Äôre creating an e-commerce website, a banking dashboard, a social media application, or an admin panel, your frontend constantly needs to send requests to a server to fetch, create, update, or delete data.
Many developers initially use the Fetch API or libraries like Axios to perform these requests. While both approaches work well for small projects, they become difficult to manage as applications grow. You often find yourself writing repetitive code for loading states, error handling, caching, refetching, request cancellation, and state management.
Imagine you have ten different pages in your application, and each page makes multiple API requests. Soon, you‚Äôll have dozens of useEffect() hooks, many useState() variables, duplicate API logic, and inconsistent error handling scattered throughout your project. Maintaining this code becomes increasingly difficult.
To solve these problems, the Redux team introduced Redux Toolkit Query (RTK Query).
RTK Query is a powerful data-fetching and caching solution built directly into Redux Toolkit. It automatically handles many of the repetitive tasks involved in working with APIs, allowing you to focus on building features instead of writing boilerplate code.
In this comprehensive guide, you‚Äôll learn everything you need to know to set up RTK Query in a React application from scratch. Every step is explained in detail so that even if you‚Äôve never used RTK Query before, you‚Äôll understand not only how to implement it but also why each step is necessary.
What is RTK Query?
RTK Query is a data fetching and caching tool that is included as part of Redux Toolkit.
Its primary purpose is to simplify communication between your React application and your backend APIs.
Instead of manually writing API functions, managing loading states, storing server responses inside Redux, handling errors, caching responses, and updating UI components, RTK Query automates these responsibilities.
You simply define your API endpoints, and RTK Query generates React Hooks for you automatically.
This means significantly less code and a much cleaner project structure.
Why Was RTK Query Introduced?
Before RTK Query, developers typically used Axios or Fetch API together with Redux.
The process usually looked like this:

Update UIAlthough this approach works, it requires writing a lot of repetitive code.
For every API request, developers usually need to:

‚ÄĘ Create async functions

‚ÄĘ Create loading state

‚ÄĘ Create error state

‚ÄĘ Store data manually

‚ÄĘ Update Redux state manually

‚ÄĘ Handle caching manually

‚ÄĘ Refetch data manually
RTK Query removes almost all of this boilerplate.
Why Should You Use RTK Query?
RTK Query provides many built-in features that simplify API management.
Some of the most important benefits include:

‚ÄĘ Automatic API caching

‚ÄĘ Automatic loading states

‚ÄĘ Automatic error handling

‚ÄĘ Automatic request deduplication

‚ÄĘ Automatic data refetching

‚ÄĘ Generated React Hooks

‚ÄĘ Optimized performance

‚ÄĘ Easy integration with Redux Toolkit
Instead of worrying about API state management, you simply focus on displaying data.
How RTK Query Works
Understanding the overall workflow will make the setup much easier.

Everything passes through RTK Query.
This allows RTK Query to cache responses, prevent duplicate requests, and automatically update your UI.
Prerequisites
Before setting up RTK Query, make sure your project already has:

‚ÄĘ React

‚ÄĘ Redux Toolkit

‚ÄĘ React Redux
If you‚Äôve followed the previous articles in this series, your project is already prepared.
Installing Required Packages
If Redux Toolkit isn‚Äôt already installed, run:
npm install @reduxjs/toolkit react-reduxLet‚Äôs understand each package.
@reduxjs/toolkit
This package provides:

‚ÄĘ configureStore()

‚ÄĘ createSlice()

‚ÄĘ createAsyncThunk()

‚ÄĘ RTK Query
It is the core Redux Toolkit library.
react-redux
This package connects React with Redux.
It provides:

‚ÄĘ Provider

‚ÄĘ useSelector()

‚ÄĘ useDispatch()
RTK Query also relies on React Redux because its generated hooks access the Redux Store.
Project Structure
A clean project structure keeps API-related code organized.

app
Contains the Redux Store configuration.
services
Contains RTK Query API definitions.
Keeping APIs inside a dedicated folder makes them reusable throughout the application.
Understanding the Setup Process
Setting up RTK Query involves five major steps:

‚ÄĘ Create an API Slice.

‚ÄĘ Register the API reducer.

‚ÄĘ Add RTK Query middleware.

‚ÄĘ Wrap React with the Provider.

‚ÄĘ Use generated Hooks inside components.
We‚Äôll explore each of these steps in detail in the upcoming articles.
Why Do We Create an API Slice?
Earlier, we learned about feature slices like:

‚ÄĘ User Slice

‚ÄĘ Counter Slice

‚ÄĘ Todo Slice
RTK Query introduces another type of slice called an API Slice.
Instead of managing local application state, an API Slice manages server data.
Its responsibilities include:

‚ÄĘ Fetching data

‚ÄĘ Caching responses

‚ÄĘ Refetching when needed

‚ÄĘ Invalidating cache

‚ÄĘ Tracking loading state

‚ÄĘ Tracking errors
Think of it as a dedicated manager for all API communication.
Understanding the Overall Architecture
Once RTK Query is configured, the architecture looks like this:

Backend ServerEvery API request follows this predictable path.
Because RTK Query sits between React and your backend, it can automatically cache responses and optimize network requests.
Common Beginner Questions
Is RTK Query a replacement for Axios?
Not exactly.
RTK Query is a data fetching and caching solution.
It uses fetch() internally by default, but it can also work with custom base queries if needed.
Many developers stop using Axios after adopting RTK Query because RTK Query already handles most of the problems Axios users solve manually.
Does RTK Query replace Redux?
No.
RTK Query is part of Redux Toolkit.
Redux manages application state.
RTK Query manages server state.
They work together seamlessly.
Can I use RTK Query without Redux Toolkit?
No.
RTK Query is built specifically for Redux Toolkit.
It depends on the Redux Store to cache and manage server data.
Should every API call use RTK Query?
In most React applications, yes.
If your application communicates frequently with a backend, RTK Query is an excellent choice because it reduces boilerplate, improves performance, and provides consistent API management.
Best Practices Before You Start
Before writing your first RTK Query API, keep these recommendations in mind:

‚ÄĘ Create one API Slice for related endpoints whenever possible.

‚ÄĘ Keep API definitions inside a dedicated services folder.

‚ÄĘ Let RTK Query manage server state instead of duplicating it in Redux slices.

‚ÄĘ Use feature slices for UI or client-side state, and use RTK Query for data that comes from the server.

‚ÄĘ Keep your API layer centralized to make maintenance easier as your project grows.
Key Takeaways
Let‚Äôs summarize what you‚Äôve learned in this introductory guide:

‚ÄĘ RTK Query is a powerful API fetching and caching solution built into Redux Toolkit.

‚ÄĘ It dramatically reduces boilerplate by generating React Hooks automatically.

‚ÄĘ It handles caching, loading states, error handling, and refetching for you.

‚ÄĘ It integrates directly with the Redux Store and works alongside your existing slices.

‚ÄĘ A clean project structure makes your API layer easier to maintain and scale.

‚ÄĘ Setting up RTK Query involves creating an API Slice, registering its reducer and middleware, and then using the generated hooks inside React components.
What‚Äôs Next?
Now that you understand what RTK Query is and why it exists, it‚Äôs time to configure it in a real React application.
In the next article, we‚Äôll build the complete setup step by step and explain every line of code.
You‚Äôll learn:

‚ÄĘ Creating your first API Slice using createApi()

‚ÄĘ Understanding fetchBaseQuery()

‚ÄĘ Registering the API reducer inside the Redux Store

‚ÄĘ Adding RTK Query middleware

‚ÄĘ Connecting everything with React

‚ÄĘ Why every line of code is necessary

‚ÄĘ Common setup mistakes and how to avoid them
By the end of the next article, your React project will be fully configured with RTK Query and ready to fetch data from real APIs.
